core_topics: AI literacy and understanding AI limits
Explorers Programme long-term aims and overview
ai_focus: Understanding AI limits and reliability (OECD K1.3)
AI can explain things clearly without understanding them
Using AI responsibly to ask questions about game development and Endstar
Testing AI suggestions against real tool behaviour in Endstar
AI predicts language patterns, not real tool behaviour
AI Literacy Focus: evaluating AI confidence versus accuracy
AI-enabled world readiness
Responsible use of AI tools in education (no AI under exam conditions)

metadata.activity_description:
Mandatory Diagnostic: Students begin by completing a mandatory diagnostic test via QR code or link. The test is not graded but required for all students and must be completed under strict exam conditions with no AI tools or web search. Results are shared by the teacher. Students will repeat this diagnostic in Lesson 24.

Lesson 1 Starter — AI Sounds Smart, But Does It Understand?: A Think-Pair-Share activity. Students ask the AI 'Explain how to build a simple game environment in Endstar', consider whether the answer sounds confident and whether confidence implies understanding, then open Endstar and test one instruction from the AI. Students prepare key points and evidence, refine with a partner, and share confidently. Key takeaway: AI can explain things clearly without understanding them — humans must test and decide. AI Literacy Focus: Understanding AI limits (OECD K1.3).

Optional Extension — Using The Studio: Students navigate to https://about.endlessstudios.com/, click 'The Studio', and log in with their allocated username and password. This activity can also be accessed outside the classroom. A video guides students through the Endless site interface and the different aspects of The Studio.

Display Settings (Step 2): Students open Endstar and log in, navigate to the settings bar at the top of the screen, and ensure settings are configured to suit their device. A video details the necessary display settings to ensure smooth software performance.

Open Endstar (Step 3): Students open Endstar on their computer, enter their allocated username and password, click 'Create a new game', name their game, click 'Create', choose their level, and re-enter the name. Two videos guide students through the User Interface layout and Tool Basics.

Main Activity — Open and Navigate Endstar: Students must be able to open Endstar, navigate all functions of the platform, and be prepared to answer questions about the platform as part of this introductory activity. This is a basic 'get to know' the Endstar programme activity.

Portfolio Evidence: Students upload their progress — what they built and how they used AI — to Classkick.

Plenary — Assessment For Learning: Four multiple-choice questions covering Game Development (GD-01.1: reducing performance issues in Endstar; GD-01.2: appropriate tool for removing an object) and AI Literacy (AI-01.1: why AI gives confident but inaccurate instructions for real tools; AI-01.2: best role for a student when using AI). OECD Mapping: Knowledge K1.3.
artifacts: Screenshot of Endstar environment uploaded to Portfolio
Portfolio Entry — Lesson 1: what was built and how AI was used
assessment_signals: Mandatory diagnostic test (pre-assessment, to be repeated in Lesson 24)
Assessment For Learning plenary — GD-01.1: which action is most likely to reduce performance issues in an Endstar environment (multiple choice)
Assessment For Learning plenary — GD-01.2: which tool is most appropriate for removing an object that is no longer needed (multiple choice)
Assessment For Learning plenary — AI-01.1: why AI can give confident instructions that do not fully work in Endstar (multiple choice, OECD K1.3)
Assessment For Learning plenary — AI-01.2: best role for a student when using AI in Lesson 1 (multiple choice)
Portfolio evidence upload to Classkick: screenshot of environment and how AI was used
Differentiated success criteria (All/Many/Some students)

lessons.lesson_title: Lesson 2: Essential Tools & Beginner Environment Design

metadata.title: Essential Tools & Beginner Environment Design
core_topics: AI literacy and critical evaluation of AI
ai_focus: AI literacy — fluency does not equal understanding (OECD K1.3)
Critical evaluation of AI-generated suggestions
Using AI to generate or refine environment design ideas
Healthy scepticism when using AI tools
Testing AI ideas inside Endstar before keeping them
Understanding AI limits in gameplay experience
learning_objectives: Use AI to generate or refine an environment idea.
Select a tile and use it to design a simple environment.
Use Endstar tools more confidently (tile, prop, move, erase).
Build and test a playable environment layout.
metadata.activity_description:
The lesson opens with an AI Literacy starter titled 'If AI Sounds Confident, Should We Trust It?' Students ask an AI tool 'What is the most important tool in Endstar and why?' and then evaluate the response critically, considering whether there is only one correct answer and whether different designers might choose differently. This is structured as a Think-Pair-Share: students think and collect key points, practice delivery with a partner, then share confidently with the class. The focus is on the principle that confidence does not always mean correctness and that human judgement still matters.

The lesson starter then has students log into Endstar, open their Level 1 project, recap tools from Lesson 1 (Move, Tile, Prop, Erase, Inspect), and identify one improvement they want to make to their environment.

A Retrieval Activity reviews all in-game tools: Empty Tool (navigation), Terrain Tool (placing terrain), Prop Tool (placing new objects), Erase Tool (removing objects), Wiring Tool (connections), Inspector Tool (changing properties), Copy Tool (duplicating objects), and Move Tool (repositioning). Students then watch an 'In-Game Tools Recap' video to reinforce tool knowledge and revise each tool's capabilities.

A class discussion follows with three questions: which tool was used most in Lesson 1 and why; how tile choice impacts how a player moves through the environment; and one way to make an environment more interesting.

The main activity requires students to create a more complex game environment in Endstar using Tiles, Props, and Move/Erase tools. Students must explain their design choices, show how the player will move across their level, and upload evidence to their Portfolio. The lesson concludes with a plenary featuring Assessment for Learning multiple-choice questions covering Game Development (GD-02.1: navigation design choices; GD-02.2: consistency in environment design) and AI Literacy (AI-02.1: AI limits in gameplay experience; AI-02.2: healthy scepticism when using AI).
assessment_signals: Assessment For Learning multiple-choice plenary questions (GD-02.1, GD-02.2)
Assessment For Learning multiple-choice plenary questions (AI-02.1, AI-02.2)
Portfolio evidence upload (screenshot)
Classkick upload of activity progress
Differentiated success criteria using All/Many/Some framework

ai_focus: AI probabilistic outputs
AI predicts patterns (OECD K1.1)
Evaluating AI outputs
AI-suggested game pathways
Limitations of AI in predicting player behaviour
AI cannot judge player frustration
learning_objectives: Understanding Basic Wiring Techniques
Testing Complex Connections
metadata.activity_description:
Starter Activity: Students are prompted with the question 'Why Does AI Give Different Answers?' They ask an AI for three ideas for a simple platformer game, compare results with a partner, and reflect on whether the AI 'thought again' or predicted something new. Key takeaway: AI predicts likelihood, not certainty, and different answers are normal.

Step 1 — Game Environment Recap: Students open Endstar and log in with their user details, open their game environment from the previous lesson, recap the work completed and tools used, then watch a video to revise tools and game environment details.

Step 2 — Basic Wiring Techniques: Students learn how Endstar lets them creatively connect objects to build mechanisms, such as a simple lever to open a door or moving an object onto a pressure plate to achieve the same result. Students are tasked to come up with innovative mechanisms to accomplish the goal of their game.

Activity 1 — Create a Complex Game Pathway in Endstar: Students create a linear pathway using connected elements. The environment should be navigable solely by moving through the links they have established. This may involve a range of door mechanisms and/or intricate puzzles.

Portfolio Evidence: Students complete activities and upload progress to Classkick. Students upload a screenshot or short demo to their portfolio.

Discussion: Whole-class discussion questions on connections — What is the most basic connection you can create? Name one way you can make this basic connection more complex. Explain a way we could make a connection very complex and difficult to solve.
artifacts: Complex game pathway built in Endstar
Portfolio screenshot or short demo of pathway
assessment_signals: Multiple choice Assessment for Learning questions on Game Development (GD-03.1, GD-03.2)
Multiple choice Assessment for Learning questions on AI Literacy (AI-03.1, AI-03.2)
Portfolio upload: screenshot or short demo of completed pathway
Classkick progress upload

metadata.title: Core Game Mechanics & Tools
core_topics: Wiring and connections in game design
ai_focus: AI output variability — AI produces different responses each time
AI lacks lasting memory or intent
Context and input wording shapes AI results
OECD K1.1: Analysing AI reliability
Testing and adjusting AI-generated puzzle ideas based on player experience
learning_objectives: Understand and Implement Basic and Complex Puzzles
metadata.activity_description:
Starter — Did the AI Change Its Mind?: Students ask an AI the same question twice ('Describe a simple platformer game environment'), compare the two outputs, and reflect on whether the AI 'changed its mind' or whether variation is expected. The activity introduces AI output variability: AI does not remember meaning or intent, different outputs are expected, and input wording shapes results.

Video Guidance — Basic Wiring: Students watch a video (Basic Wiring) to understand the tools needed to make a basic connection, how to create a basic connection, and how to create a more complex connection.

Step 1 — Creating Puzzles: Students are introduced to the concept that every great game involves thoughtful challenges. They consider what types of challenges they could create using different props in Endstar to enhance player engagement and creativity.

Video Guidance — Creating a Basic Puzzle: Students watch a second video to understand different props available and how to set up a very basic puzzle.

Activity 1 — Create an Engaging Puzzle: Students explore all available prop options in Endstar to design an engaging and interactive puzzle. The puzzle must incorporate multiple inputs, encouraging diverse and meaningful problem-solving strategies. Using basic props, students create a puzzle that feels complex and enjoyable to solve, testing players' logic and creativity.

Portfolio Evidence: Students complete the activities and upload their progress to Classkick, including a screenshot or short demo of their puzzle.

Plenary Discussion: Students discuss three questions — what props can be used to create a basic puzzle, one way to make a more complex puzzle, and why the puzzle should engage the player further into the game's story.
artifacts: Interactive puzzle built in Endstar using basic props
Screenshot or short demo of puzzle uploaded to portfolio (Classkick)
assessment_signals: Portfolio evidence: screenshot or short demo uploaded to Classkick
Assessment For Learning (AFL) multiple choice plenary questions on game development (GD-04.1, GD-04.2)
Assessment For Learning (AFL) multiple choice plenary questions on AI literacy (AI-04.1, AI-04.2)
Differentiated success criteria (all/many/some students)

lessons.lesson_title: Lesson 5: Market Research and Target Audience

metadata.title: Market Research and Target Audience
ai_focus: AI literacy — bias and data limits (OECD K1.4)
Audience context in AI outputs
AI-generated market insights and their limitations
Using AI to summarise game reviews
Comparing AI reasoning with human reasoning
AI depends on the data it was given
learning_objectives: How to complete market research on selected games
Learn the basics of Market Research as an Entrepreneur
metadata.activity_description:
Starter — 'Does Data Always Tell the Truth?' (Slide 4): Students look at two different platformer games and decide which would be more popular and why, considering whether they are guessing or using real information. They then ask an AI: 'Which of these games would be more popular, and why?' and compare the AI's reasoning with their own. Focus is on data bias, data limits, and audience context (AI Literacy: OECD K1.4).

Activity 1 — 'Complete Market Research on Two Platformer Games' (Slide 10): Students research and select two platformer games. Using their workbooks, they conduct thorough market research on both games — identifying target audience, listing main features, analysing what makes each game successful, collecting peer feedback, and compiling data. All research notes must be detailed and recorded in the workbook to guide their game creation process.

Discussion — 'Let's Discuss' (Slide 16): Students respond to three reflection questions: (1) What is the most useful tool as I create a game and why? (2) How would you use this tile in a larger, more complex environment? (3) What tiles would be complementary to the tile you chose and why?

Portfolio Evidence (Slide 11): Students complete all activities and upload their progress to Classkick.
artifacts: Market research notes recorded in workbook
assessment_signals: Portfolio evidence upload to Classkick (formative)
Tiered success criteria: all students / many students / some students
AFL plenary multiple-choice: GD-05.1 — Why is copying features from a popular game risky?
AFL plenary multiple-choice: GD-05.2 — Which source best helps understand player preferences?
AFL plenary multiple-choice: AI-05.1 — AI summarises game reviews; what should students check first?
AFL plenary multiple-choice: AI-05.2 — Why might AI-generated market insights be incomplete?
Research notes recorded in workbook

The lesson closes with Portfolio Evidence completion (Slide 20), where students document what they built and how they used AI, uploading their progress to Classkick. Discussion questions are used throughout the lesson (Slides 17 and 24) to facilitate class conversation about PMF and team collaboration.

metadata.title: Crafting a Go-To-Market Strategy
ai_focus: Responsible use of AI in communication (OECD K1.4)
AI-generated marketing messages and their limitations
Critical evaluation of AI output for audience fit
AI optimises for language quality, not intent or audience fit
Human review and adjustment of AI-generated content
Avoiding uncritical adoption of AI suggestions without testing and refinement
learning_objectives: Understanding Go-To-Market (GTM)
Making your Go-Market-Strategy
metadata.activity_description:
Starter Activity (Slide 3): Students ask an AI to write a short marketing message for their game. They then think critically about who the message is aimed at and what assumptions it makes about the player. Finally, they edit or rewrite the message so it better fits their target audience. The activity reinforces that AI can draft messages, but humans must decide if they actually work.

Step 1 — Understanding GTM (Slides 6–12): Students learn the five core components of a Go-To-Market strategy through direct instruction and class discussion: Target Market, Value Proposition, Distribution Channels, Pricing Strategy, and Sales Strategy. A UAE pearl merchant scenario is used as the contextual example — students imagine being a pearl merchant in the UAE who has discovered glowing pearls and must plan how to sell them globally. Structured class discussion prompts explore: which audience (tourists vs. locals) would be most interested, how to craft a compelling value proposition story, how to choose a distribution channel (local market in Dubai, online store, or luxury jewelry stores), and how to plan sales tactics (market display, online ads, exclusive previews).

Activity 1 — Making your team game's GTM (Slides 13–14): As a team, students begin planning the launch of their game. They craft a Go-To-Market strategy aligned with their game's theme and objectives, thinking about who their ideal players are, what message will grab attention, and how to make the game's introduction an exciting event.

Plenary (Slides 17–19): Assessment For Learning multiple-choice quiz questions on GTM concepts (GD-07.1, GD-07.2) and AI Literacy (AI-07.1, AI-07.2). Closing discussion questions prompt reflection on the importance of team-based GTM planning, how to delegate GTM responsibilities within a team, and whether one GTM element is more crucial than the others.
assessment_signals: Portfolio Evidence — students document what they built and how they used AI (Slide 15)
Progress upload to Classkick
Assessment For Learning quiz: GD-07.1 — What is the primary goal of a Go-To-Market (GTM) strategy?
Assessment For Learning quiz: GD-07.2 — Which element is most important when defining a target audience?
Assessment For Learning quiz: AI-07.1 — What does an AI marketing message attracting the wrong audience demonstrate?
Assessment For Learning quiz: AI-07.2 — Which action best demonstrates responsible AI use in marketing?

metadata.title: Leveraging Marketing Channels for Game Launch
ai_focus: Using AI to recommend marketing platforms
Questioning AI recommendations for reach versus relevance
Ethics and responsibility in AI-driven audience targeting
Risk of relying solely on AI for marketing channel selection
AI Literacy: OECD K1.4 — Ethics, responsibility & audience awareness
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of marketing channels for a game launch.

metadata.activity_description:
Starter (Slide 3): Students ask an AI the question 'What is the best platform to market a game to teenagers?' and then think critically about who might be excluded by the AI's recommendation and whether reach and impact are the same thing. Students then decide on a marketing channel and explain why it is appropriate, not just effective.

Activity 1 — Build a Channel Strategy (Slide 7): Students choose one or more marketing channels (e.g., Instagram, TikTok) to promote their game. They design a sample post or advertisement for one of the selected channels and explain why they chose that particular channel and why this strategy would be most effective for their game launch.

Activity 2 — Develop a Marketing Plan (Slide 12): Students review a provided sample marketing plan and then create a marketing plan for a different game, which could be one of their own creations. Students are encouraged to be creative and think outside the box. Completed work is uploaded to Classkick.

Plenary (Slides 15–17): Students complete Assessment For Learning quiz questions on game development marketing (GD-08.1, GD-08.2) and AI literacy (AI-08.1, AI-08.2), followed by a class discussion on why they chose their channels, how those channels align with their target audience, and what challenges they might face.
artifacts: Sample post or advertisement for a chosen marketing channel

ai_focus: Evaluating AI-generated story ideas for quality and fit
Understanding that AI produces generic patterns from common training data
Student role in evaluating, adapting, or rejecting AI suggestions based on intent and meaning
AI as a tool for story ideation, not a replacement for creative judgement

learning_objectives: How to brainstorm a game idea

metadata.activity_description:
Starter Activity: Students look at a short game clip or screenshot provided by the teacher. They think about what is happening first and what challenge the player faces, then discuss how game stories differ from stories in books or films.

Activity 1 – Plan your Story: Students collaborate as a team to plan and creatively communicate their game's storyline. They can use Mural (online) or physical sticky notes. The team outlines the story by defining the beginning (how to hook the player), the middle (the world and setting), the challenges/villains (who or what opposes the hero), and the ending (ultimate goal or resolution). Students record the outline in the workbook.

Activity 2 – Create Your Game as a Comic: Students use the student workbook to draw key scenes and challenges from their game in a comic strip format. They add dialogues and short descriptions to each scene to bring the story to life, clearly outlining the game's progression and sharing their creative vision.

Plenary Discussion: Three discussion questions are posed — why it is important to plan your story, what types of challenges make a game engaging, and how students intend to develop a story that ties challenges together.

Comic strip storyboard (drawn in student workbook)

metadata.title: Creating a Gameplay Loop
core_topics: AI as an assistant in game design
ai_focus: Algorithmic transparency in reward systems
AI as an assistant for designing gameplay loops
Prompting AI (Ana) to explain, design, and refine a three-step gameplay loop
AI literacy: analysing reliability of AI-generated outputs (OECD K1.1)
Evaluating and refining AI-generated game design ideas based on player experience
learning_objectives: Create a gameplay loop for your game idea
How to create a gameplay loop with emotion and instinct
metadata.activity_description:
Starter Activity (Slide 3): Students play a short game segment or imagine a familiar game, focusing on what makes it engaging. They think about what action the player performs repeatedly and what immediate reward or feedback follows, then sketch a simple loop illustrating the relationship: Action → Reward → Progress.

AI as an Assistant Activity (Slide 10): Students prompt Ana (AI) to explain what a core gameplay loop is in a platformer, design a simple three-step loop with action, reward, and progression for their chosen game theme, and suggest one twist to add risk–reward tension. Students then sketch and share their final loops.

Activity 1 – Create a Gameplay Loop (Slide 13): Building on their game idea from the previous session, students use PowerPoint or paper to create a gameplay loop emphasising four main points: Action (what is the player doing?), Reward (what reward do they receive?), Expansion (how does the game change?), and 6-11 Framework (what emotions and instincts is the loop based on?).

Endstar Activity (Slide 18): Students open Endstar and create their gameplay loop within the platform.

Plenary Discussion (Slide 19): Class discusses three questions — what is a gameplay loop, how to enhance and develop one, and why creating an engaging gameplay loop enhances a game's depth and story.

Plenary Discussion (Slide 19): Class discusses three questions — what is a gameplay loop, how to enhance and develop one, and why creating an engaging gameplay loop enhances a game's depth and story.

teacher_notes.notes: On games we control the characters, and we tend to personify their emotions; we feel what they feel, or we make them react as if they felt what we feel. We have 6 basic emotions: Fear, Pride, Anger, Joy, Happiness and Sadness.
Teaching Note: Have students complete the activities and then upload their progress to classkick.

metadata.title: Crafting Engaging Game Narratives
ai_focus: Explainable AI — creating AI that people can easily understand and trust
Human creativity and authorship vs. AI-generated suggestions (OECD K1.3)
Using AI as a creative assistant to generate story hooks from gameplay loops
Knowing when NOT to use AI — narrative meaning and emotion require human judgement
Multi-step AI prompting for creative tasks
learning_objectives: Create a gameplay loop for YOUR game idea?
What is a Game Narrative?
metadata.activity_description:
Starter — 'Whose Story Is It Anyway?': Students write one sentence describing their game's world or main character, then reflect on who the story is for and what emotion they want the player to feel. Optionally, students ask AI to 'Suggest a story idea for this type of game' and compare it with their own idea, discussing the difference between AI suggestions and human-decided meaning and emotion.

Activity 1 — Creating a Game Narrative: Based on their own game idea, students develop a full narrative for a platformer-style game. Using Mural, they plan all key elements including their character's backstory, game world, conflict, choices, consequences, and narrative progression.

AI as an Assistant Activity: Students use a three-step AI prompting sequence to craft a story hook from their gameplay loop — (1) 'Using this loop, [action] → [reward] → [progression], write a one-sentence story hook.', (2) 'Suggest one surprising character or setting detail to make that hook more memorable.', (3) 'Combine the hook and twist into a single, exciting sentence.' Students then share their combined hook with the class and discuss which concept most excites them.

Portfolio Evidence: Students complete the activities and upload their progress to Classkick.

metadata.title: Level Design
core_topics: AI as an Assistant in Game Design
ai_focus: Responsible AI design (fairness, transparency, accessibility for all skill levels)
AI as an assistant for drafting and refining level concepts
Prompting AI to generate full level blueprints with defined routes, obstacles, and landmarks
Iterative AI refinement with targeted prompts
OECD K1.1: Knowing when NOT to use AI (early spatial planning stages)
learning_objectives: Create a Level Layout for your Game
Use AI to draft and refine a detailed level concept, defining routes, obstacles, and landmarks, to build clear, engaging platformer layouts.
metadata.activity_description:
Starter: Students sketch a top-down or side-view map of a game level on paper. They think about where the player starts and where challenges and safe spaces are, then add notes explaining why each area exists and what purpose it serves in the player's journey.

AI Activity: Students use AI as an assistant to draft and refine a detailed level concept. They prompt AI to generate a full level blueprint, identifying start/end points, main and optional routes, checkpoints, and obstacles, then refine their design with targeted AI tweaks. Example prompts include creating a themed platformer level with defined routes and combat arenas, adding hidden shortcuts, and suggesting memorable landmarks.

Activity 1 — Create a Simple Level Layout: Students sketch a top-down or side-view map of a level, highlighting key points with notes. Key elements to include: Start and End Point / Gameplay Loop, highlighted pathways, and annotated obstacles and rewards.

Endstar Activity: Students open Endstar and begin building their level layout based on their sketched design.

Plenary — Discussion: Students discuss the following questions: Why is a gameplay loop important? How do you make a player's journey strategically engaging? What instincts and emotions did you focus on, and why?
artifacts: Top-down or side-view level layout map (hand-sketched)
Annotated level map with notes explaining key areas
assessment_signals: Assessment For Learning multiple-choice questions (GD-12.1, GD-12.2, AI-12.1, AI-12.2)
Portfolio evidence upload (level layout map with annotations)
Classkick upload for progress tracking
Tiered success criteria (all students must / many students will / some students may)

metadata.title: Foundations of Color Theory
ai_focus: Digital wellbeing and AI: how game colour palettes affect player mood, focus, and screen time
Using AI to learn and explore colour theory concepts (complementary vs. analogous palettes, mood palettes)
AI prompt engineering for colour design: generating palettes with hex codes and mood justifications
AI-generated quiz format for colour theory revision
Evaluating AI outputs: judging whether AI-suggested palettes match the intended player experience (OECD K1.3)
AI literacy: understanding that AI optimises for visual appearance rather than emotional or design intent
metadata.activity_description:
Starter Activity (Slide 4): Students look at different game environments and consider how each colour palette makes them feel (calm, tense, or exciting). They then discuss how colour might affect player behaviour, with a focus on colour theory and visual communication (OECD K1.3).

Ask AI – Digital Wellbeing & AI (Slide 5): Students consider how game colour palettes and visual effects can affect player mood, focus, and screen time. Guiding questions ask how colour choices could support players taking breaks or feeling calm, and what happens when colours are too bright or overwhelming.

Main Activity – Create an Environment of Color (Slide 16): Students open Endstar and use its tools to design a small part of their game level, applying the principles of colour theory. They make intentional choices about tileset colours, scenery and setting, and prop choices. The goal is to create an immersive environment that is either warm and welcoming or cold and tranquil.

AI as an Assistant (Slide 18): Students run one of their own colour-theory prompts with AI — for example, asking AI to explain complementary and analogous colours, suggest palettes with hex codes for a spooky forest, or quiz them on colour theory with multiple-choice questions. They take notes on the definitions or examples AI provides and share which explanation style helped them most.

Assessment For Learning (Slides 20–21): Students complete multiple-choice formative assessment questions on game design colour usage (GD-13.1, GD-13.2) and AI literacy in colour selection (AI-13.1, AI-13.2).

Portfolio Evidence (Slide 17): Students upload their progress to Classkick as portfolio evidence for Lesson 13.
artifacts: Game level environment designed in Endstar applying colour theory principles
assessment_signals: Bi-Weekly Student Feedback Form (Slide 3)
Portfolio evidence upload to Classkick (Slide 17)
Differentiated success criteria — All/Many/Some framework (Slide 19)
Formative multiple-choice assessment GD-13.1: What is the primary purpose of using colour intentionally in a game environment?
Formative multiple-choice assessment GD-13.2: Which colour choice is most likely to create tension or danger in a level?
Formative multiple-choice assessment AI-13.1: AI suggests a colour palette that looks attractive but does not match the game's mood. What does this show?
Formative multiple-choice assessment AI-13.2: What is the student's responsibility when using AI for colour inspiration?

teacher_notes.notes: The color wheel is a visual representation of the primary, secondary, and tertiary colors. The primary colors are red, blue, and yellow. When mixed, they can create secondary colors, which are orange, green, and purple. Tertiary colors are created by mixing a primary and secondary color together. For example, red-orange or blue-green.

The color wheel is a visual representation of the primary, secondary, and tertiary colors. The primary colors are red, blue, and yellow. When mixed, they can create secondary colors, which are orange, green, and purple. Tertiary colors are created by mixing a primary and secondary color together. For example, red-orange or blue-green.

Complementary colors are pairs of colors that are opposite each other on the color wheel. The primary complementary colors are: Red and green, Blue and orange, Yellow and purple. When placed next to each other, complementary colors create a strong contrast and enhance each other's intensity. They can be used to create dynamic and visually interesting designs.

Color temperature refers to how warm or cool a color is, depending on where it's located on the color wheel. Warm colors, such as reds, oranges, and yellows, can be used to convey warmth, energy, and excitement. They can also be used to create a sense of intimacy or comfort. Cool colors, such as blues, greens, and purples, can be used to convey calmness, serenity, and stability. They can also be used to create a sense of distance or detachment.

metadata.activity_description:
Starter Activity (Slide 3): Students are prompted with the question 'Where Do Good Visual Ideas Come From?' They collect images, colours, shapes, or textures that match their game idea, think about what the references have in common and what mood or style they suggest, and optionally compare their own references with AI-generated suggestions for visual inspiration.

Main Activity – Creating a Moodboard (Slide 8): Teams create a moodboard to inspire the design of their game, with Endstar open during the activity. Focus areas include: other similar games that inspire design choices, a colour palette, character visuals, and scenery/environment images. Students upload their completed moodboard to their portfolio.

AI as an Assistant Activity (Slide 10): Students compare peer feedback with AI feedback on their moodboard. They collect peer strengths and suggestions, then use provided AI prompts to obtain three strengths and two improvement suggestions from an AI. Students identify where feedback overlaps or differs and plan one combined revision. Example prompts provided: 'Here's my moodboard description for a [theme] platformer: [paste description]. Give me three strengths and two improvement suggestions.'; 'Which of my peer's feedback points match your suggestions, and where do you offer something new?'; 'What one extra change would you make to tie my moodboard more closely to my game's theme?'

Classkick progress upload

teacher_notes.notes: A mood board in video game design is a visual collage that captures the game's overall aesthetic and tone. It serves as inspiration and communication tool, aligning the team's vision with themes, colors, and styles through images, textures, and design elements. Components typically include concept art, character designs, and color palettes that set the mood. The mood board acts as a reference throughout development, ensuring consistency and cohesion in the game's visual elements.

lessons.lesson_title: Lesson 15: Introduction to Rule-Blocks on Endstar

metadata.title: Introduction to Rule-Blocks on Endstar
ai_focus: AI and cybersecurity: AI can detect cyber attacks faster than humans
AI can also be used by hackers to find weaknesses in systems
AI used in online games to catch cheaters or hackers
Using AI as an assistant to explain Lua, identify real-world uses in games/apps, and compare scripting languages
AI can hallucinate — caution required when using AI to suggest rule logic
Best practice: use AI for ideas, then test and refine logic manually
Analysing reliability of AI-generated information (OECD K1.1)
learning_objectives: Basics of Lua
How to use Lua in Endstar
metadata.activity_description:
Starter Activity (Slide 3): Students watch or review a simple rule-block example provided by the teacher. They think about what the rule controls and what would happen if one part changed, then recreate the rule and test one change. Focus: systems thinking and rule logic (OECD K1.1).

AI as an Assistant (Slide 12): Students use AI to demystify Lua — what it is, where it's used, and why game developers choose it over other languages. Students prompt AI to: (1) explain Lua in simple terms for someone who has never coded, (2) list five popular games or apps that use Lua and give one reason each chose it, and (3) compare Lua to Python and JavaScript. Reflection questions include which Lua fact surprised them most and whether they would choose Lua or another language for a game mod.

Activity 1: Basic Rule-Block Setup (Slide 13): Students watch the attached video on basic rule-block wiring, then recreate the wiring and connections from the video. More advanced students are encouraged to design a more complex system using additional rule-blocks. A free Codecademy Lua course is provided for students interested in deeper exploration of the language.

Portfolio Evidence (Slide 14): Students complete the activities and upload their progress (screenshot or short demo) to Classkick as portfolio evidence of what they built and how they used AI.

Plenary / Discussion (Slide 20): Whole-class discussion on three questions: how Lua makes it easier for developers to add new features, how Lua scripts allow player personalization, and how Lua's simple coding style speeds up development compared to other languages.

Portfolio entry (screenshot or short demo uploaded to Classkick)

Formative multiple-choice question AI-15.1: Why should students be cautious when using AI to suggest rule logic?
Formative multiple-choice question AI-15.2: What is the best use of AI when working with rule blocks?

Plenary (Slide 17): Class discussion on how many students chose each path, what the team prioritised and why, and how advanced scripting can enhance gameplay or user experience in Endstar.

lessons.lesson_title: Lesson 17: Conducting Peer Testing for Insights

metadata.title: Conducting Peer Testing for Insights
ai_focus: AI as an assistant for refining and structuring peer feedback
Comparing AI-generated feedback with human peer feedback
Human judgement combined with AI support in decision-making
Prompt engineering: writing prompts to rewrite raw notes into rubric-aligned feedback
Evaluating AI outputs (OECD K1.3)
Future of work: collaboration between humans and AI
Blending AI and human insights to strengthen critique skills
learning_objectives: Understand what peer testing is
Analyse each other's games by peer testing them
metadata.activity_description:
Starter — Does Your Game Work for Someone Else?: Students play another team's game and reflect on what was easy to understand and what was confusing or frustrating. They record one strength and one clear improvement in their workbook. The focus is honest, actionable feedback.

Activity 1 — Time to Test: Students pair up with a member from another team (not a friend) to conduct serious peer testing. Using specific headings in their workbook, they observe and record feedback across four areas: Usability, Functionality, Gameplay Balance, and Narrative/Engagement.

AI as an Assistant: Students use AI to refine their raw peer-testing notes into structured, rubric-aligned feedback using prompts such as: 'Here are my raw notes on Team X's game under Usability, Functionality, Balance, and Narrative: [paste notes]. Rewrite them into three strengths and two improvement suggestions aligned to our rubric.' and 'Summarize all our feedback into a brief report for the teacher, highlighting key insights and how they map to the lesson's learning outcomes.' Students compare AI-crafted feedback with human insights and reflect on how to blend both perspectives.

Portfolio Evidence: Students complete activities and upload their progress to Classkick.

Plenary — Let's Discuss: Whole-class reflection on three questions: (1) What did I learn from testing another game? (2) What piece of feedback did I receive that was very valuable? (3) Is there any feedback you received that you do not agree with? Why?
artifacts: AI-refined structured feedback (three strengths and two improvement suggestions)
assessment_signals: Formative multiple-choice Assessment for Learning questions on Game Development (GD-17.1, GD-17.2) and AI Literacy (AI-17.1, AI-17.2)
5-point rubric assessing Usability, Challenge/Design, Gameplay Balance, and Narrative and Engagement
Workbook recording of peer-testing observations and feedback
Portfolio evidence uploaded to Classkick
Bi-Weekly Student Feedback Form (https://forms.gle/ihCECsXvADJqfB6x6)

lessons.lesson_title: Lesson 18: Incorporating Feedback into Game Design

metadata.title: Incorporating Feedback into Game Design
core_topics: Feedback categorization (usability, functionality, gameplay balance, narrative)
ai_focus: AI regulation and international governance differences as a real-world analogy for prioritization
Using AI to organize and categorize peer feedback into themes
Using AI to identify high-impact and feasible game improvements
Drafting student reflections with AI assistance
AI literacy — understanding AI limitations in context-based and intent-based decision-making (OECD K1.1)
AI as a decision-support tool rather than autonomous decision-maker
learning_objectives: Analyse and implement the received feedback into your game
metadata.activity_description:
Starter Activity — What Should We Fix First? (Slide 3): Students review the feedback their game received, think about which issues affect the player experience most and which can realistically be fixed, then decide on one improvement to focus on. The emphasis is on smart iteration rather than fixing everything.

Activity 1 — Putting Feedback in Place (Slide 8): Students use the class to carefully evaluate the feedback they received on their game by following a five-step evaluation process: (1) Organize Feedback by categorizing comments into usability, functionality, gameplay balance, etc.; (2) Identify Patterns by looking for recurring feedback to highlight priority issues; (3) Assess Feasibility based on resources and alignment with the game's vision; (4) Prioritize Changes by focusing on highest-impact fixes such as bugs or critical usability issues; (5) Test & Iterate by implementing changes, testing, and seeking further feedback. Students are reminded to prioritize the most important changes first and continually test their game.

AI as an Assistant (Slide 10): Students work with AI to turn scattered play-test notes into an organized plan. They categorize feedback into core areas (usability, functionality, balance, narrative), identify the most impactful and feasible changes to make before the next prototype, and craft a clear reflection on why they chose those improvements. Three suggested AI prompts are provided: one for sorting feedback into categories and spotting themes, one for identifying the two highest-impact and realistic suggestions, and one for drafting a student-friendly reflection.

Portfolio Evidence (Slide 9): Students document what they built and how they used AI, then upload their progress to Classkick.

Plenary — Let's Discuss (Slide 14): Class discussion around three questions: How did you evaluate your feedback? What did you choose to implement from this? What did you find most difficult about evaluating your feedback?
artifacts: Portfolio entry documenting what was built and how AI was used
Workbook highlighting three or more key changes implemented
assessment_signals: Portfolio Evidence — students document what they built and how they used AI, uploaded to Classkick
Differentiated success criteria rubric (all/many/some students)
Assessment For Learning multiple-choice questions on iteration in game development (GD-18.1, GD-18.2)
Assessment For Learning multiple-choice questions on AI literacy OECD K1.1 (AI-18.1, AI-18.2)
Plenary discussion questions on feedback evaluation and implementation choices

lessons.lesson_title: Lesson 19: Validate and Demonstrate Market Demand

metadata.title: Validate and Demonstrate Market Demand
core_topics: AI literacy — analysing reliability and ethical responsibility
ai_focus: AI literacy: analysing reliability and ethical responsibility (OECD K1.4)
Fact-checking AI answers — identifying when AI may be wrong or based on poor sources
Using AI to generate neutral, unbiased survey questions
Using AI to explain product-market fit in gaming
Using AI to identify demand patterns and flag biased feedback from survey responses
learning_objectives: What market demand is and why it matters.
How to validate demand before launching a product.
How to demonstrate that a market exists for your product.
metadata.activity_description:
Starter — 'Does Your Game Fit Its Players?': Students review feedback, interviews, or tester comments they have already received. They think about patterns or trends, what players enjoy or struggle with, and decide whether their game matches its intended audience. The slide notes that data informs decisions but does not make them.

Ask AI – Fact-Check Mode: Students are prompted to use AI in fact-check mode, asking AI for three quick ways to fact-check its answers and how to recognise when a confident answer may be based on poor or missing sources.

Activity 1 – Time to Validate: Students team up and work on validating market demand for their own product ideas (or teacher-selected ideas). Step 1: Design a simple survey or interview script. Step 2: Conduct 5–10 interviews or survey 20+ potential customers. Step 3: Compile and analyse the results, identifying key trends or insights about market demand.

AI as an Assistant: Students work with AI to design a simple, ethical survey or interview script tailored to their game concept; ask AI to explain product–market fit in gaming and how to recognise it; then feed AI actual survey or interview responses to spot key demand patterns and avoid bias. Suggested prompts are provided: writing five neutral, inclusive survey questions; explaining product–market fit; and identifying top demand patterns from pasted data.

Portfolio Evidence: Students complete activities and upload progress to Classkick.

Plenary – Let's Discuss: Three reflection questions — (1) Was I able to effectively show the demand for my game? (2) Where are my gaps compared to competitors? (3) What was the most important thing I learned through this exercise?
assessment_signals: Multiple-choice Assessment for Learning questions: GD-19.1 (what evidence best validates product-market fit), GD-19.2 (which result suggests good product-market fit)
Multiple-choice AI Literacy questions: AI-19.1 (why to be cautious using AI to analyse survey results), AI-19.2 (student responsibility after AI highlights trends)
Graded rubric (1–5 scale) with categories: Research and Data, Customer Feedback, Demonstration of Demand
Portfolio evidence upload to Classkick

metadata.title: Structuring a Compelling Presentation
core_topics: Using visuals and infographics to support data
AI-assisted pitch preparation and time management
ai_focus: Using AI to refine wording or structure while preserving human intent
Risk of AI controlling entire presentation narrative — message may not reflect team's true intentions
AI-assisted time management: breaking a 3-hour pitch session into time-boxed blocks
AI-assisted role assignment based on team strengths
AI simulation of audience Q&A for pitch rehearsal
OECD K1.3: Using AI with traditional tools
learning_objectives: The importance of structuring a presentation clearly and logically.
Creating a presentation that engages and informs the audience.
Using visuals and storytelling to enhance your presentation.
metadata.activity_description:
Starter Activity: Students review their existing data/slides presentation and consider whether it clearly explains the problem, journey, and solution. They make one change that improves clarity or impact. Focus is on good presentations telling a clear story rather than just listing features.

Activity 1 — Create Your Presentation: As a team, students use a presentation tool to outline their game's concept, market demand, and features using the provided template. Every team member has an assigned role. The presentation must include the problem, journey, and solution, and students must include at least 5 visuals to support their data.

AI as an Assistant: Students work with AI to break a 3-hour pitch session into three 1-hour blocks (planning, slide design, rehearsal), assign roles based on team strengths (speaker, writer, designer, timekeeper), and simulate likely audience questions. AI is used to draft confident responses for Q&A practice and to generate a time-boxed schedule.

Discussion Activities: Two class discussions — the first comparing text vs. visual formats using a social media data example and deciding what to make visual vs. textual in their own presentation; the second a reflective discussion on how they chose visuals, what was most effective, and what they would change.
artifacts: Team game presentation/pitch deck (minimum 3 sections, 5 slides, 5 visuals)
AI-generated time-boxed pitch schedule with role assignments
assessment_signals: Portfolio evidence upload to Classkick (Slide 12)
Assessment For Learning plenary MCQ — Game Development: GD-20.1, GD-20.2 (Slide 15)
Assessment For Learning plenary MCQ — AI Literacy OECD K1.3: AI-20.1, AI-20.2 (Slide 16)
Rubric with 6 categories: Structure & Organization, Team Collaboration, Content, Storytelling, Visuals & Design, Presentation Skills — scored 1–5 points each (Slide 17)
Class discussion reflections on presentation choices (Slides 10, 18)

metadata.activity_description:

Step 1 — Finalizing the team's game (Slide 7): Students go through every part of their game carefully, checking that props, challenges, and the game's story work as intended, and fix any issues before the showcase.

metadata.title: Polishing Your Pitch
core_topics: AI as a critical review assistant
ai_focus: Using Ana (AI assistant) as a critical friend to review Showcase presentation for clarity, flow, and checklist coverage
AI-assisted comparison of slide outline and speaking script against the presentation checklist
AI simulation of audience Q&A to prepare students for live questions
AI limitations: less reliable for predicting audience questions due to lack of real-time audience context and intent
Responsible AI use for question preparation — guessing likely questions then refining responses through practice (OECD K1.3)
learning_objectives: Review your presentation checklist
Start creating your 'Showcase' presentation
metadata.activity_description:
Lesson 22 opens with a starter activity (Slide 3) where students answer questions about their game from another group or their teacher, practising communication under pressure. Students then reflect on whether the questions revealed gaps in their explanation or game design, and adjust their responses, refine their pitch, or update their slides accordingly.

The primary activity (Activity 1, Slide 10) is to finalise the Showcase presentation. Students carefully follow a provided template and make any necessary additions to ensure the presentation is engaging, clear, and ready to deliver. Emphasis is placed on collaboration and delegation as skills that mirror professional game development.

A structured AI activity (Slide 7) has students use Ana (the AI assistant) as a 'critical friend' to conduct a full end-to-end review of their pitch. Students prompt Ana to compare their slide outline and speaking script against the lesson's presentation checklist, identify gaps or unclear sections, suggest improvements to engagement and coherence, and simulate audience Q&A. Each team member confirms their speaking role before delivery. Example prompts are provided: asking Ana what is missing or unclear from the slide outline, whether the sequence flows well and who speaks when, and how to make the conclusion slide more powerful.

Students also review a detailed presentation checklist (Slides 8–9) covering five areas: Game Story and Engagement, Testing and Feedback, Product Market Fit, Go-To-Market Strategy, and Presentation Preparation.

The lesson concludes with a class discussion (Slide 15) on three questions: what is the most crucial element of a successful presentation, how to delegate presentation aspects across the team, and how to overcome public speaking nervousness.
assessment_signals: Portfolio evidence upload to Classkick (slide 11 teacher note)
Assessment For Learning quiz — Game Development: GD-22.1 (why audience questions are valuable during pitch practice), GD-22.2 (best response when a question highlights a weakness)
Assessment For Learning quiz — AI Literacy: AI-22.1 (why AI is less reliable for predicting audience questions), AI-22.2 (how AI can support question preparation responsibly)
OECD K1.3 mapped assessment: Choosing when NOT to use AI
